coredata.dc:title: One-pot room-temperature direct synthesis of bovine serum albumin-based fluorescent carbon nanoparticles

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to Ma. Christian Albor (CIO) for their technical assistance on the SEM analysis, to the Laboratorio de Caracterizaion de Materiales (CIO), we also thanks to National Laboratory of Nano and Biomaterials (CINVESTAV, Merida, Yuc. Mex.) for their facilities (TGA and XPS), Dr. G Ramos for fluorometer access (CIO), and Mario Ruiz Berganza (CIO) for proofreading the manuscript.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis Absorbance spectra of the FC-NPs were obtained for different concentrations of NaOH. Inset shows absorbance at 535 nm and QY fluorescence as a function of NaOH concentration.
FC-NPs samples photograph (top) and fluorescence emission spectra of sample A4 for the different excitation wavelengths (below).
The figure shows fluorescence emission maps for samples A1, A4, A5 and A8. The white “x”s in the maps indicate the location of maximum emission for each excitation wavelength.

UV–Vis Absorbance spectra of the FC-NPs synthesized at different concentrations of the GA. The inset shows the absorbance at the 535 nm principal band and the fluorescence QY as a function of the GA volume.
Fluorescence emission maps for samples G1 to G5: the white “x”s indicate the emission maximums for each excitation wavelength.

Time-resolved fluorescence decay raw data fitted with a two-exponential; continuous lines represent the best fit. The inset table shows the calculated fast, slow, and mean weighted fluorescence lifetimes.
Fourier transform infrared spectra of samples A1, A4, A5 and A8.
XPS spectra of as-synthesized FC-NPs show carbon and oxygen, nitrogen and sodium peaks of samples A1, A4, A5 and A8.
Electron microscopy analysis of sample A4 at different magnifications (a, b and c panels. Size histogram of the FC-NPs is shown in panel c).
Epifluorescence images recorded by using FC-NPs as a marker: a) oral cell, b) onion cell, c) Acanthamoeba castellanii, and d) Pestalotiopsis spp. Fungi.
The table shows a summary of the relative percentage values of C, O, N and Na determined through XPS.

head.ce:title: One-pot room-temperature direct synthesis of bovine serum albumin-based fluorescent carbon nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Departamento de Biología, División de Ciencias Naturales y Exactas, Universidad de Guanajuato, Noria Alta S/N Col. Noria Alta, C.P. 36050, Guanajuato, Guanajuato, México

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (FC-NPs) with a mean size of 5.5 nm were fabricated at room temperature by means of a novel one-pot direct synthesis method, using bovine serum albumin (BSA), as a biomass-based precursor, and glutaraldehyde in a mixture of water/ethanol (1:4 v/v) under alkali conditions. This novel method shortens the times of synthesis and purification of the nanoparticles, and does not require thermal energy, since no calcination is needed for the nanoparticles production, and the waste of reactants and solvents is minor. The FC-NPs displayed two emission peaks: one around 450 nm, and the second at 555 nm, being the later the most intense for wavelength excitations from 450 nm; fluorescence quantum yields values varied from 11.6 to 27 %, depending on their synthesis conditions, and in fact, the solutions emit a strong green-yellow fluorescence when excited with 532 nm. The FTIR spectra indicated that the main functional groups present were -NH2 and -COOH. XPS analysis confirmed the FTIR results and showed that carbon dots were composed mainly of C, N, O and Na. Finally, we tested the fluorescent carbon nanoparticles as cell biomarkers in human, plant cell lines and microbial cultures, achieving good results for this application.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent nanoparticles (F-NPs) are emerging as the future alternative materials for bioimaging, biosensing and even in photovoltaic devices. Compared to traditional fluorescent dyes, carbon NPs exhibit improved optical properties: enhanced fluorescent emission, long-term stability to photobleaching, higher quantum yields, light harvesting efficiency, good water miscibility, and biocompatibility [1]. Although the parameters like light harvesting efficiency are calculated principally when complex organic sensitizers are used in the design of photovoltaic devices [2–4], quantum yield is calculated when fluorescent materials are used as markers mainly for sensing or biosensing. For example, quantum dot nanoparticles (Qdots) can be made of semiconductors, noble metals or magnetic transition metals that exhibit different emission colors with a narrow optical line spectrum [5,6]. The noble metal nanocluster made from gold, silver and copper has a unique structure of a metal-core protected with an organic ligand shell; the ligand on the surface of these nanoclusters influences their structures and changes their optical properties [7–10]. Organic dyes, polymers and conjugated fluorescent molecules have been used to obtain F-NPs, generally coated with organic or inorganic materials to make them water-soluble in order to enhance fluorescence emission [11–13].

On the other hand, fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (FC-NPs, carbon dots) are the newest type of carbon-based nanomaterials, doped with hetero atoms like oxygen, nitrogen and sulfur so that they display tunable fluorescence emission and excitation properties [14,15]. Usually, organic molecules, organic solvents, sugars, and other biomass sources are used to synthesize FC-NPs, and albumin-like proteins have been reported as a good biomass precursor for FC-NPs. Bovine serum albumin (BSA) is a macromolecule that consists of 583 amino acid sequences; it is rich in carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur, and when irradiated with an excitation wavelength of 280 nm, it exhibits autofluorescence with a low quantum yield (QY) of 0.132 % [16]. Previous works have reported the use of albumins as biomass sources for the synthesis of FC-NPs using different preparation protocols: for example, Jilin Tang et al. prepared FC-NPs via a simple hydrothermal method using bovine serum albumin (BSA) as a carbon source in the presence of surface passivation reagents with a QY of 11 % [17]. Xueqin An et al. prepared carbon dots using albumin (obtained from the egg) by the alkali hydrolysis method under ambient temperature, the method was able to obtain carbon dots with a QY of 16.8 % [18]. Jing Wang et al. used edible chicken eggs to obtain FC-NPs using plasma-induced pyrolysis [19], Qingxiu Yang got BSA FC-NPs by carbonization (stainless steel autoclave, 200 °C) of freshly synthesized BSA nanoparticles in deionized water [20], Shui Wee et al. synthesized fluorescent carbon dots via simple acid hydrolysis of bovine serum albumin [21], and Pichardo et al. reported a solvothermal method that requires the preliminary preparation of BSA-NPs as a precursor and transfers it to ethylene glycol at 200 °C [22]. Additionally, new methodologies that eliminate aggressive solvents and carry out the synthesis at room temperatures and with a minimum of sample preparation will be ideal for new materials for bio-sensing and bio-imaging applications.

This work reports a novel but straightforward one-pot room-temperature direct synthesis method to prepare FC-NPs using BSA as the primary biomass source in a solution of water/ethanol at different alkali conditions with the addition of glutaraldehyde, along with their optical characterization. We found out that the change in pH after adding NaOH allows us to obtain FC-NPs, thus avoiding the calcination step or the use of aggressive solvents [20,21]. The alkali conditions seem to be one of the main factors that permit obtaining FC-NPs [20], but it is still unclear if glutaraldehyde is essential in the synthesis or if this reagent improves the fluorescence emission. For this reason, in this work, we are interested in optimizing the synthesis parameters, NaOH and glutaraldehyde concentrations, to improve the optical properties of the FC-NPs. Furthermore, we show a preliminary result for their use as a fluorescent marker in cell cultures and microbial pathogens.

Materials: bovine serum albumin (BSA, 96 %), Sodium hydroxide (98 %), Glutaraldehyde (25 % solution in water), and Ethyl alcohol (99.5 %) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich and used as received. All the water used in the experiments was obtained from a MilliQ© Direct system.

Briefly, our one-pot direct synthesis method consists in mixing 8 mL of a BSA stock solution (0.5 % M/V in water), adding 1 mL of NaOH solution (at a certain concentration) under vigorous magnetic stirring, followed by the dropwise injection of 32 mL of ethanol at a 10 mL/min rate, using a home-made syringe pump. Finally, a given volume of glutaraldehyde (GA, 25 %) solution is added to the mix and allowed to react for 5 min; the magnetic stirrer is then removed, and the solution is placed in a rotary shaker for 12 h.

To understand the role of NaOH in the synthesis, we prepared a set of nine samples following the protocol described above with slight modifications. Each solution was prepared by adding 8 mL of a BSA stock solution to a 50 mL sterilized centrifuge tube under vigorous magnetic stirring, and mixing it with 1 mL NaOH (0, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4, 0.7, 1 M) solution; then, 32 mL of ethanol were injected dropwise at a 10 mL/min, and finally, 160 μL of GA (25 %) were added. Samples were labeled as A0 to A8.

To study the impact of GA in the FC-NPs synthesis, a set of 6 samples was prepared, modifying only the concentration of GA. According to our previous analysis, the synthesis was carried out as previously described, fixing the NaOH concentration at 0.2 M, and the selected volumes of GA were 0, 40, 80, 160, 320, and 640 μL; the samples were labeled as G0 to G5, respectively.

After the synthesis, samples were purified for their corresponding analysis (Fluorescence quantum yield, lifetime and emission maps) in the following way: ethanol content was eliminated by heating the samples at 60 °C for 8 h, and then centrifuging at 10,000g for 30 min to eliminate the bigger non fluorescent particles. The supernatants were transferred to glass vials to dry them at 80 °C. Finally, 10 mg of each sample powder was dispersed in 5 mL MilliQ water by sonication.

The Pestalotiopsis spp. fungi were cultured at 28 °C in Potato Dextrose Agar (PDA, 20 % peeled and sliced potato, 1.0 % glucose and 1.5 % agar) growth medium for eight days. After that, the fungus was suspended in PBS buffer and FC-NPs for 30 min.

Onion epidermal cells were obtained using forceps; we took a thin onion strip and placed on the glass slide. As soon as the cells were prepared, FC-NPs were taken to stain with the onion epidermal cells.

Acanthamoeba castellanii (T4 genotype) was cultured at 30 °C without shaking in proteose peptone yeast extract glucose (PYG) growth medium in a 25 cm2 tissue culture flask. After that, trophozoites of A. castellanii (2.5 × 105 cell/mL) were induced to cysts by cultivating them in encystation buffer (95 mM NaCl, 5 mM KCl, 8 mM MgSO4, 0.4 mM CaCl2, 1 mM NaHCO3, 20 mM Tris-HCl, pH 9.0) at 37 °C by 48 h. The trophozoites and cysts were fixed with a 4 % paraformaldehyde plus 0.05 % glutaraldehyde solution for 20 min, and then washed with PBS (Phosphate Buffer Solution). Finally, the trophozoites and cysts were collected for staining with FCNPs.

Human oral epithelial cells were isolated from healthy volunteer donors' oral cavities before the subjects brushed their mouths and rinsed out with plenty of water. The non-invasive method of obtaining buccal cells consists in rubbing cheeks against wood swabs to exfoliate cells. Freshly collected buccal cells were smeared onto a glass slide, and then fixed using the heat fixation method for later staining. Subsequently, to develop a staining assay with FC-NPs, the different cells were incubated for 30 min at room temperature after being rinsed with PBS buffer to eliminate all free nanoparticles. The samples were observed with a fluorescence microscope (DMLS Leica), using an excitation wave of 400 nm. Image acquisition was made with an AxioCamICc1 camera (Carl Zeiss).

The UV–Vis spectra of FC-NPs dispersed in water were acquired using an Agilent Carry 5000 UV–Vis spectrophotometer, from 300 to 650 nm, using quartz cuvettes of 1 cm in path length. Measurements of photoluminescence excitation-emission maps, absolute quantum yield and lifetime were carried out on an Edinburg Instruments FS5 fluorometer. Fluorescence excitation-emission maps were acquired with 1 nm and 4 nm bandwidths for excitation and emission, respectively. Excitation varied from 300 to 550 nm, and emission spectra were acquired up to 700 nm, starting with a 10 nm offset from the excitation wavelength. The integrating sphere method was used to measure the photoluminescent quantum yield (QY), with an excitation bandwidth of 6 nm, and emission of 0.2 nm. Fluorescence lifetime measurements were carried out using a 530 nm pulse laser as an excitation source, and the time-resolved emission was measured at 555 nm. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra from 4000 to 400 cm−1 were acquired using a Cary660 FTIR spectrometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded on a K-AlphaTM + X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer System. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was carried out in a JEM-2100 JEOL microscope operated at an accelerated voltage of 200 kV. TEM images were recorded using a Gatan CCD digital camera.

As mentioned in Section 2.1, a set of FC-NPs was prepared by controlling NaOH concentration in the precursor solution. Sample A0, prepared by adding 1 mL of water instead of NaOH, resulted in a turbid solution after the addition of GA, indicating that the use of NaOH is essential to get FC-NPs. Fig. 1 shows the UV–Vis absorbance spectra acquired for the set of samples, from A1 to A8, synthesized with NaOH. All the samples showed high absorption below 400 nm. Besides, a small absorption band is observed at around 535 nm for samples A2-A8. The inset plot of Fig. 1 shows the absorbance at 535 nm as a function of NaOH concentration. Absorbance increases monotonically up to 0.3 M of NaOH, and after that, absorbance decreases up to 1 M concentration.

Samples A1 to A8 showed excellent colloidal stability, exhibiting a color that varies from yellow to a reddish hue as NaOH concentration increases; see Fig. 2 . Fig. 2 also presents the characteristic emission spectra of sample A4 when the excitation wavelength varied from 300 to 550 nm in 10 nm steps. All the samples had a similar entrancing emission behavior, but emission changed significantly from sample to sample; see fluorescence excitation-emission maps in Fig. 3 . The excitation-emission spectral information is condensed as color maps; an emission spectrum can be seen as the variation in color along the x-axis for a given excitation wavelength, while the excitation spectrum can be appreciated as the variation of color along the y-axis. The FC-NPs showed tunable excitation behavior upon excitation at 300–550 nm range. Sample A1 had a maximum emission at 540 nm when excited with 490 nm, while all other samples, A2 to A8, have a maximum emission at 555 nm with a 540 nm excitation.

Fluorescence quantum yields (QY) were measured by the integrating sphere method, fixing the excitation at 530 nm. The corresponding measurements of QYs are shown in the inset of Fig. 1. The QY of samples A1, A4, A5, and A8 were 16.6, 27.1, 24.6, and 11.6 %, respectively. Sample A5 had the maximum optical absorption, and its emission peak was observed at 555 nm for all the wavelengths of excitation. On the other hand, sample A4 had the highest QY, and for this reason, we considered it to be the optimal sample and used it as a reference sample to evaluate the role of GA in the synthesis of the FC-NPs.

The sample prepared without GA did not produce FC-NPs; it formed a colloid that only exhibited optical scattering. On the other hand, the UV–Vis absorbance spectra of samples G1 to G5, prepared with different concentrations of GA, are shown in Fig. 4 . All samples presented high absorption below 400 nm and a small absorption band at 535 nm, which varied in intensity. Samples G1 to G4 presented QY above 20 % and only in sample G5, the one with the maximum content of glutaraldehyde, QY decreased to 13.2 % (see insert in Fig. 4).

The fluorescence excitation-emission maps showed a maximum emission at 555 nm, obtained for excitation at 540 nm for all samples (Fig. 5 ). Samples G1 and G2 correspond to samples with the lower GA content, and their emission peak was fixed at 555 nm for the entire range of excitation wavelengths. Our results show that both NaOH and GA are necessary to obtain FC-NPs from BSA, but NaOH concentration is the main factor determining the quality of the fluorescence emission properties. For instance, by using the appropriate NaOH concentration (0.2 M), we can prepare FC-NPs either by lowering or increasing the concentration of GA, and still, we get FC-NPs with QYs over 20 %.

Besides, photoluminescent decay lifetimes were measured for samples A1, A4, A5, and A8, using a 530 nm picosecond pulse laser with a repetition rate of 1 MHz. Characteristic fluorescence decays are shown in Fig. 6 ; the curves were fitted by two exponentials, and the mean lifetime was calculated using the equation below:
T m
=
I 1
T 1 2
+
I 2
T 2 2
I 1
T 1
+
I 2
T 2
,
where T
m is the amplitude-weighted average lifetime of fluorescence, T 1 is the time constant of the fast decay process, T 2 is the time constant of the slow decay process, which corresponds to surface states, and I 1 and I 2 are the corresponding fractional values of the fluorescence intensities. The calculated fluorescence lifetimes are shown in the inset table of Fig. 6, and the average lifetimes decreased as NaOH concentration increased.

Furthermore, FC-NPs were analyzed utilizing Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to investigate the presence of the functional groups of the FC-NPs. Fig. 7 shows the principal infrared vibrational modes; for example, a broad absorption peak located around 3294 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching mode of -OH, while the peaks at 2937 and 2861 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching modes of -CH2 and -CH3 [23,24]. Besides, samples A8 and A5 show well-defined absorption bands at 3420 and 3352 cm−1 assigned to stretching modes of (−NH2), a small peak at 1650 cm−1 assigned to the stretching mode of =CO (amide I), one more between 1540 and 1560 cm−1 assigned to the bending mode of -NH bending and the stretching mode of -CN (amide II), and a band located between 1396 and 1420 cm−1 assigned to the stretching mode of –CN and the bending mode of -NH (amide III) [25]. The FTIR results confirm the presence of methylene, carbonyl and amino groups.

XPS analysis of as-synthesized FC-NPs of samples A1, A4, A5, and A8 are shown in Fig. 8 . The survey scan of the samples shows that the FC-NPs are mainly composed of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen, as was confirmed by FTIR; besides, XPS analysis detected the presence of sodium. Table 1 shows the relative percentage values of the elements found in the analyzed FC-NPs.

We observed that when the concentration of NaOH increases in the synthesis, the content of carbon and nitrogen of samples A1 to A8 decreases, but the relative percentage of oxygen and sodium content increases; however, sample A4 has the highest QY according to the optical characterization. Its preparation does not require a high content of NaOH or GA, and its relative carbon content is 67.0 %, oxygen 16.4 %, nitrogen 5.4, and sodium 11.2 %.

Sample A4 was analyzed in a high-resolution electron transmission microscope (HRTEM). Fig. 9a and b shows the characteristic TEM images of sample A4. A lattice spacing of 0.21 nm can be seen in panel c of the same figure and on the particle size histogram, with a mean size of 5.5 nm shown in panel d.

FC-NPs are marked by multicolor emission profile, small sizes, low cytotoxicity, high biocompatibility and excellent photostability, making them a good option for fluorescence bioimaging or biosensing. Epifluorescence imaging was carried out in human oral cells, onion cells, Acanthamoeba castellanii and Pestalotiopsis spp. fungi to evaluate the performance of FC-NPS as a fluorescent biomarker. Fig. 10 shows the fluorescence image for oral and onion cells, Acanthamoeba castellanii and Pestalotiopsis spp. fungi. The image of human oral epithelial cells (Fig. 10a) showed that FCNPs entered the cells and went to the cytoplasm and nucleus, while in onion cells, FCNPs were found in the nucleus, cytoplasm, and even between cell walls.

On the other hand, when A. castellanii was incubated with FC-NPs, trophozoites showed that the FC-NPs went only to the contractile vacuoles, but in the case of cysts, the FCNPS entered everywhere. Finally, when Pestalotiopsis spp. fungi were incubated with FC-NPs, they penetrated the apical and basal appendages, and in hyaline end cells, the fluorescence produced by the internalization of FCNPs is detectable.

Here, we report a fast and straightforward one-pot room-temperature synthesis of FC-NPs using BSA protein as a biomass source. FC-NPs were synthesized under alkali conditions, controlling NaOH concentration in each solution and using glutaraldehyde as a cross-linker. Following this protocol, we were able to get FC-NPs with an average size of 5.5 nm, with a maximum fluorescence emission located at 555 nm, and with QY up to 27.1 %; glutaraldehyde and NaOH are both indispensable to get the fluoresce carbon nanoparticles. This new protocol not only shortens the times of synthesis and purification, but also requires less energy, since no calcination is needed, and produces less waste of reactants and solvents.

JL Pichardo-Molina, and PE Cardoso-Avila contributed to conception and design, contributed to acquisition, analysis, and interpretation, drafted the manuscript, and critically revised the manuscript. R J Patakfalvi, L Aparicio-Ixta, F Pedro-García, H J Ojeda-Galvan, J C Villagómez-Castro, and L L Flores-Villavicencio contribute to analysis and interpretation and critically revised the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The author(s) declared no potential conflicts of interest with respect to the research, authorship, and/or publication of this article.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to Ma. Christian Albor (CIO) for their technical assistance on the SEM analysis, to the Laboratorio de Caracterizaion de Materiales (CIO), we also thanks to National Laboratory of Nano and Biomaterials (CINVESTAV, Merida, Yuc., Mex.) for their facilities (TGA and XPS), Dr. G Ramos for fluorometer access (CIO), and Mario Ruiz Berganza (CIO) for proofreading the manuscript.
